T1001.desc: Adversaries may obfuscate command and control traffic to make it more difficult to detect.
T1001.name: Data Obfuscation

T1001.001.desc: Adversaries may add junk data to protocols used for command and control to make detection more difficult.

T1001.002.desc: Adversaries may use steganographic techniques to hide command and control traffic to make detection efforts more difficult.
T1001.002.name: Steganography

T1001.003.desc: Adversaries may impersonate legitimate protocols or web service traffic to disguise command and control activity and thwart analysis efforts.
T1001.003.name: Protocol or Service Impersonation

T1002.desc: An adversary may compress data (e.g., sensitive documents) that is collected prior to exfiltration in order to make it portable and minimize the amount of data sent over the network.

T1003.005.desc: Adversaries may attempt to access cached domain credentials used to allow authentication to occur in the event a domain controller is unavailable.

T1003.007.desc: Adversaries may gather credentials from the proc filesystem or `/proc`.

T1003.008.desc: Adversaries may attempt to dump the contents of /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow to enable offline password cracking.

T1007.desc: Adversaries may try to gather information about registered local system services.

T1008.desc: Adversaries may use fallback or alternate communication channels if the primary channel is compromised or inaccessible in order to maintain reliable command and control and to avoid data transfer thresholds.

T1009.desc: Adversaries can use binary padding to add junk data and change the on-disk representation of malware without affecting the functionality or behavior of the binary.

T1010.desc: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of open application windows.

T1011.desc: Adversaries may attempt to exfiltrate data over a different network medium than the command and control channel.

T1011.001.desc: Adversaries may attempt to exfiltrate data over Bluetooth rather than the command and control channel.

T1012.desc: Adversaries may interact with the Windows Registry to gather information about the system, configuration, and installed software.

T1013.desc: A port monitor can be set through the   API call to set a DLL to be loaded at startup.

T1014.desc: Adversaries may use rootkits to hide the presence of programs, files, network connections, services, drivers, and other system components.

T1016.002.desc: Adversaries may search for information about Wi-Fi networks, such as network names and passwords, on compromised systems.

T1018.name: Remote System Discovery

T1021.005.desc: Adversaries may use Valid Accounts to remotely control machines using Virtual Network Computing (VNC).

T1023.desc: Shortcuts or symbolic links are ways of referencing other files or programs that will be opened or executed when the shortcut is clicked or executed by a system startup process.

T1025.desc: Adversaries may search connected removable media on computers they have compromised to find files of interest.

Some adversaries may split communications between different protocols.

T1027.desc: Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

T1027.010.desc: Adversaries may obfuscate content during command execution to impede detection.

T1027.011.desc: Adversaries may store data in "fileless" formats to conceal malicious activity from defenses.

T1027.013.desc: Adversaries may encrypt or encode files to obfuscate strings, bytes, and other specific patterns to impede detection.

T1027.014.desc: Adversaries may utilize polymorphic code (also known as metamorphic or mutating code) to evade detection.

T1027.015.desc: Adversaries may use compression to obfuscate their payloads or files.

T1027.016.desc: Adversaries may use junk code / dead code to obfuscate a malware’s functionality.

T1037.005.desc: Adversaries may use startup items automatically executed at boot initialization to establish persistence.

T1039.desc: Adversaries may search network shares on computers they have compromised to find files of interest.

T1040.desc: Adversaries may passively sniff network traffic to capture information about an environment, including authentication material passed over the network.

T1041.desc: Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel.

T1558.002.name: Silver Ticket
